Non CES Production Functions I know CES production functions dominate economics, but I was curious, why? I've never seen a research paper or presentation utilize any form of a production function that is not CES.  why is imposing CES so important in our models? While CES (Constant Elasticity of Substitution) production functions are widely used in economics due to their analytical tractability and flexibility—allowing for varying degrees of substitutability between inputs—not all production relationships in reality conform to the CES structure. Non-CES production functions, such as Leontief (fixed proportions), Cobb-Douglas (unit elasticity), and translog (flexible functional form), are sometimes overlooked because CES can nest several important special cases while maintaining mathematical simplicity, especially in general equilibrium models. Researchers often impose CES because it provides closed-form solutions, stable comparative statics, and easier estimation, making it a “go While Constant Elasticity of Substitution (CES) production functions are widely used in economics due to their mathematical tractability and ability to flexibly model substitution between inputs, they are not the only functional forms available. Alternatives, such as Leontief (fixed-proportions), Cobb-Douglas (unit elasticity), and translog (flexible functional form) production functions, also exist. However, CES dominates research because it offers a neat balance between generality and analytical simplicity: its single elasticity parameter directly captures how easily inputs can be substituted, and its structure allows straightforward aggregation and estimation. Non-CES production functions—such as Cobb-Douglas, Leontief (fixed proportions), and translog—exist and are occasionally utilized. However, CES functions are favored because they provide a simple, consistent framework for varying substitution elasticities, making calibration and comparative statics manageable. Imposing a CES structure aids researchers in deriving closed-form solutions and clear policy insights. In contrast, non-CES forms may lack analytical convenience or may not While Constant Elasticity of Substitution (CES) production functions are widely used in economics due to their flexibility and analytical tractability, they are by no means the only functional forms available. Non-CES production functions include the Cobb-Douglas, Leontief, and translog forms, each with distinct properties regarding factor substitutability and returns to scale. The dominance of CES in research arises because it provides a straightforward way to model varying degrees of input substitution while retaining mathematical simplicity and closed-form solutions, which are valuable for both theoretical and empirical analysis. Imposing a CES structure often streamlines estimation and comparative statics, whereas non-C While CES (Constant Elasticity of Substitution) production functions are widely used in economics due to their analytical tractability and ability to flexibly model substitution between inputs, many alternative forms exist, such as Cobb-Douglas, Leontief, and translog production functions. CES functions often dominate empirical and theoretical work because they offer a simple, parameter-driven way to capture varying degrees of input substitutability, ease of aggregation, and convenient mathematical properties (like closed-form solutions). In contrast, non-CES functions may be less common in research because they can lead to greater computational complexity, reduced interpretability, or lack of reliable estimation techniques

Did Capitalism and Adam Smith Support a Central Bank?  Does Adam Smith and Capitalism support a Central Bank/Federal Reserve?  I am trying to read through the book "Wealth of Nations" to understand. Having a central/regulatory figure dictate supply and interest rates, seems less laissez faire. However curious if Adam Smith has any documentation on this subject. Adam Smith, in "The Wealth of Nations," advocated for a largely laissez-faire economy, emphasizing free markets and limited government intervention. However, Smith acknowledged the usefulness of certain financial institutions, including banks, for economic stability and growth. He recognized the Bank of England as a central bank of his era but cautioned against excessive government influence over the money supply and credit. Smith did not explicitly endorse a central bank with expansive regulatory powers, as seen in the modern Federal Reserve. Instead, he warned that government manipulation of currency could harm economic confidence. In summary, while Smith recognized the practicality of a stable national bank, he did not fully advocate for Adam Smith’s foundational work, *The Wealth of Nations* (1776), advocates for free markets and limited government intervention—principles central to classic capitalism. Smith did not explicitly endorse the idea of a modern central bank like the Federal Reserve, which manages money supply and interest rates. However, he did address the role of banks in issuing currency and cautioned against unrestricted banknote issuance, suggesting some regulatory oversight was necessary to prevent instability. Smith recognized that governments have a role in maintaining financial stability but was wary of excessive central control, preferring a competitive banking system. Thus, while classic capitalism and Adam Smith emphasize laissez-faire approaches, they Adam Smith, considered a foundational thinker in capitalism, discussed banking in his book "The Wealth of Nations" (1776). While Smith believed in limited government intervention and valued free markets (laissez-faire), he did not directly advocate for a modern central bank like the Federal Reserve. Instead, Smith acknowledged the potential problems caused by unregulated banks issuing excessive paper money, which could lead to instability. He supported some government oversight to maintain monetary stability and prevent reckless banking practices but stopped short of endorsing a central authority to control money supply or set interest rates. Smith favored free markets and minimal government intervention, arguing that the “invisible hand” of self-interest often allocates resources efficiently. However, he acknowledged the utility of banks for facilitating commerce and discussed the dangers of excessive note issuance by private banks, suggesting some regulation was necessary to maintain stability. Capitalism, as influenced by Smith, generally prefers decentralized decision-making, but Smith himself saw some limited government role in regulating banking practices to prevent widespread financial instability. Smith was generally wary of government involvement in economic affairs, believing that the "invisible hand" of the market would best regulate supply and demand. However, he did recognize certain necessary roles for the state, such as maintaining public order and enforcing contracts. Is it always a trade off between efficiency and equity?  Is there any situations where we can achieve both equity and efficiency? I'm thinking of Covid 19 vaccine program which is run by Goverment. Although the cost for the program is paid by the money from taxes, it still helps with economic growth right? (in my mind, it's like if people are saved they will have a normal life and buy stuffs, which will boost the production) Or is there anything wrong in my mind? The relationship between efficiency and equity is often framed as a trade-off, where policies that promote fairness might reduce economic efficiency, and vice versa. However, this is not always the case. For instance, government-run Covid-19 vaccine programs offer an example where both efficiency and equity can be achieved. By ensuring equitable access to vaccines, the government not only protects the most vulnerable populations but also promotes public health overall. This widespread protection reduces the spread of the virus, enabling economic activity to resume and fostering economic growth. The cost of such programs, funded by taxes, can be justified by the long-term economic benefits: healthier populations are more productive, It is a common belief in economics that there is often a trade-off between efficiency (maximizing total output) and equity (fair distribution of resources). However, this trade-off is not absolute. Certain policies can advance both goals at once. The government-run Covid-19 vaccine program is a good example. By providing free vaccines funded by taxes, the policy promotes equity because everyone, regardless of income, can access life-saving medicine. At the same time, widespread vaccination increases public health, reduces disruptions to the workforce, and encourages consumer spending, which boosts economic growth—enhancing efficiency. Thus, in situations like effective public health interventions, The relationship between efficiency and equity is often seen as a trade-off; policies designed to promote fairness can sometimes reduce overall economic output, and vice versa. However, this trade-off is not inevitable. Public health programs like the Covid-19 vaccine rollout demonstrate how equity and efficiency can align. When governments provide vaccines to everyone, regardless of income, it ensures equitable access. At the same time, widespread vaccination protects public health, prevents costly outbreaks, and enables economic activity to resume. As people return to work and normal life, production and consumption increase, promoting economic growth. In such cases, equity-enhancing policies can also improve efficiency, showing that The relationship between efficiency and equity is often seen as a trade-off; policies that promote fairness can sometimes reduce economic efficiency, and vice versa. However, this trade-off is not universal. For example, government-led Covid-19 vaccine programs demonstrate that achieving both goals is possible. By providing vaccines to all citizens free of charge, the government promotes equity by ensuring equal access, regardless of income. At the same time, widespread vaccination minimizes illness, enabling people to return to work, boosting consumer spending, and supporting economic recovery—thereby increasing efficiency. Thus, in certain cases, especially in public health, policies can simultaneously advance equity and efficiency, While economic policy often involves a trade-off between efficiency (maximizing total wealth) and equity (ensuring fairness), there are situations where both can be achieved. The Covid-19 vaccine program is a good example. By providing vaccines free of charge, the government ensures equitable access, especially for vulnerable populations. At the same time, widespread vaccination improves public health, allowing economies to reopen and productivity to recover. This boosts consumer confidence, increases spending, and supports economic growth. Thus, government intervention can sometimes enhance both equity and efficiency, rather than forcing a choice between the two. Have the deregulation measures that caused the 2007-2008 crisis been rolled back?  The 2007-2008 financial crisis was largely attributed to several deregulation measures, especially the Gramm-Leach-Bliley act. Have any of these measures been rolled back after the crisis, or new ones introduced to prevent a similar crisis? Have the deregulation measures that caused the 2007-2008 crisis been rolled back?  The 2007-2008 financial crisis was largely attributed to several deregulation measures, especially the Gramm-Leach-Bliley act. Have any of these measures been rolled back after the crisis, or new ones introduced to prevent a similar crisis? After the 2007-2008 financial crisis—often linked to deregulation, especially the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act that repealed the Glass-Steagall separation of commercial and investment banking—there were significant efforts to re-regulate the financial sector. The most notable response was the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010. Dodd-Frank introduced stricter oversight of banks, created the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, increased capital requirements, and aimed to limit risky trading practices. While these measures aimed to address some causes of the crisis, many provisions have been weakened or rolled back in recent years, Following the 2007-2008 financial crisis, many policymakers recognized that earlier deregulation—especially the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act of 1999, which repealed parts of the Glass-Steagall Act and allowed banks to engage in both commercial and investment banking—had played a role in increasing systemic risk. In response, the United States passed the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act in 2010. Dodd-Frank introduced stricter oversight of financial institutions, created the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, and imposed regulations designed to limit risky practices. While the core provisions of Gramm-Leach-Bliley remain, Following the 2007-2008 financial crisis, some deregulatory measures blamed for the turmoil, such as those from the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act, were not fully rolled back. Instead, new regulations were introduced to address the weaknesses revealed by the crisis. The most significant of these is the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010, which imposed stricter oversight on banks, set up new consumer protections, limited certain speculative activities (like proprietary trading through the Volcker Rule), and increased transparency in derivatives markets. While Dodd-Frank strengthened oversight, some of its rules have been softened in After the 2007-2008 financial crisis, which was partly blamed on deregulation measures like the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act that allowed banks to engage in riskier activities, the U.S. government implemented major reforms to increase oversight and stability in the financial system. Chief among these was the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010, which introduced stricter regulations on banks, enhanced consumer protections, and established new agencies such as the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB). While Dodd-Frank did not completely reverse deregulation measures like the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act, it did impose significant In response to the 2007-2008 financial crisis, which was linked to deregulation measures such as the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act of 1999, the U.S. government introduced significant new regulations rather than fully rolling back previous laws. Most notably, the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act was enacted in 2010 to address systemic risks and prevent future crises. Dodd-Frank imposed stricter oversight on banks, increased consumer protections, and established new regulatory bodies. However, the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act itself—which allowed the merger of commercial and investment banks—has not been repealed.

Bootstrap always valid under asymptotic Normality?  If an estimator is known to have an asymptotically normal distribution, is that sufficient for the bootstrap to be valid?  It seems that is must be, but in 20 minutes of Googling I have come up empty on a proof. Bootstrap always valid under asymptotic Normality?  If an estimator is known to have an asymptotically normal distribution, is that sufficient for the bootstrap to be valid?  It seems that is must be, but in 20 minutes of Googling I have come up empty on a proof. While asymptotic normality of an estimator is an important property, it is not by itself sufficient to guarantee the validity of the bootstrap. Validity of the bootstrap generally means that the bootstrap distribution consistently approximates the true sampling distribution of the estimator. For many estimators, especially the sample mean under i.i.d. sampling, both asymptotic normality and bootstrap validity hold. However, there are important cases—such as estimators for the sample median or dependent data structures—where an estimator may have an asymptotically normal distribution, but the naive (nonparametric) bootstrap can fail to provide a consistent approximation of the The validity of the bootstrap for an estimator with asymptotic normality is not guaranteed solely by the normality itself. While asymptotic normality (i.e., the estimator, properly scaled, converges in distribution to a normal distribution) is a necessary condition in many bootstrap contexts, it is not sufficient for bootstrap validity. The bootstrap aims to approximate the distribution of the estimator by resampling from the data; for this approximation to be valid, additional regularity conditions, such as smoothness of the functional, continuity of the variance, and certain moment conditions, are often required. In particular, if the estimator's distribution is sensitive to The bootstrap is a powerful tool for estimating the sampling distribution of an estimator, but asymptotic normality alone does not guarantee its validity. While it is true that if an estimator is asymptotically normal, the bootstrap often approximates its sampling distribution well, this is not universally sufficient. The validity of the bootstrap also depends on other factors, such as smoothness and stability of the estimator with respect to the underlying empirical distribution, and the absence of irregularities like discontinuities. Some estimators, such as sample quantiles or ones involving boundary parameters, may be asymptotically normal yet not permit valid bootstrap inference. In summary While asymptotic normality of an estimator is often associated with the validity of the bootstrap, it is *not* generally sufficient by itself to guarantee that the bootstrap will provide valid inference. Asymptotic normality means that, as sample size increases, the distribution of the estimator approaches a normal distribution. However, for the bootstrap to be valid, the bootstrap distribution must consistently estimate the actual sampling distribution of the estimator. This requires certain regularity conditions, such as smoothness and continuity of the underlying population distribution, and conditions on the estimator's functional form (e.g., being a smooth function of empirical moments). There are classic examples Asymptotic normality of an estimator means that, as the sample size increases, the distribution of the estimator approaches a Normal distribution. However, this property alone is **not** sufficient to guarantee the validity of the bootstrap. The validity of the bootstrap depends more fundamentally on whether the empirical (bootstrap) distribution can consistently approximate the sampling distribution of the estimator. While many asymptotically normal estimators do admit valid bootstrap procedures—especially under conditions like independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) samples and smooth estimating functions—there are important exceptions. Has monopoly theory incorporated network effects as a source of monopoly?  I studied industrial organization for my Econ. Ph.D. four decades ago. At that time industrial organization had no way to incorporate 'network effects' into monopoly theory. With the advent of social media (especially Facebook) since then accusations of monopoly power against social media are heard daily. Has someone articulated a theory of network effects as the source of monopoly?  Many thanks. Yes, the field of industrial organization has since incorporated network effects as a key source of monopoly power. Since the 1980s and 1990s, economists have developed models showing that in markets where a product’s value increases with the number of users—such as social media platforms, telecommunication networks, and operating systems—network effects can create significant barriers to entry. These positive feedback loops can lead to “winner-takes-all” or “natural monopoly” outcomes even without traditional cost advantages. Landmark contributions by economists like Michael Katz, Carl Shapiro, and Jean Tirole have formalized how network effects sustain monopoly power, making Since the 1980s, industrial organization theory has significantly evolved to incorporate network effects as a source of monopoly power. While traditional monopoly theory focused on cost structures, barriers to entry, and product differentiation, economists now recognize that some firms—especially digital platforms like Facebook—derive market dominance from network effects: the value of a product increases as more people use it. Network effects occur when a product or service becomes more valuable as more people use it, creating positive feedback that can lead to market dominance. Modern industrial organization literature, especially since the 1990s, has formalized these effects in models explaining why digital platforms—where users’ participation directly increases value for others—can develop persistent, sometimes unassailable, monopolies. Early classical models did not account for these effects, but the rise of digital platforms and social media, such as Facebook, prompted economists to formalize how network effects can create and sustain monopolies. Scholars have developed models showing that strong network effects lead to market "tipping," where one firm can capture most users and deter entry by rivals, even absent traditional cost or legal barriers. If you need to perform constrained optimization of a general-form algebraic equation with symbolic variables—rather than plugging in specific numbers—most basic online calculators will be limited, as they often require explicit values for parameters. However, more advanced symbolic computation platforms such as **Mathematica (Wolfram Language)** and **SymPy (a Python library)** support symbolic constrained optimization. For example, you can use Mathematica’s `Maximize` and `Minimize` functions with symbolic constraints. Is there a name for relative diminishing returns, or relative increasing opportunity cost?  Consider the following hypothetical situation:  You are producing something where the total amount produced, 𝐴 , is equal to the product of two factors, 𝑋  and 𝑌 . Hence 𝐴=𝑋∗𝑌 .  𝑋  and 𝑌  both cost $1 to increase by 1.  In this situation, 𝑋  is not said to have diminishing returns, because holding other factors constant, 𝑋  has linear returns. Increasing 𝑋  by 10% will increase output by 10%, and increasing 𝑋  by 100% will increase output by 100%.  𝑋  is also not said to have increasing opportunity cost, because the opportunity cost of increasing 𝑋  by 1 is equal to increasing 𝑌  by 1, regardless of how many 𝑋  or 𝑌  you have.  By symmetry, the same arguments are true for 𝑌 .  However, if 𝑋=20  and 𝑌=10  (and 𝐴=200 ), then by reducing 𝑋  to increase 𝑌 , for the same cost, you can increase output by having 𝑋=15  and 𝑌=15 , so 𝐴=225 . Also, by spending $1 on 𝑋 , you would increase output by 5%, while by spending $1 on 𝑌 , you would increase output by 10%.  In general, increasing one factor will increase the returns of the other factor, and by extension, diminish the relative value of that factor (or increase the opportunity cost in terms of the output, but not in terms of dollars or the alternative purchase).  This situation is very common in video games, and because investing too heavily into either 𝑋  or 𝑌  would be inefficient, people will often say that both 𝑋  and 𝑌  have diminishing returns. However, I've found this to be very misleading, because it implies that 𝐴  increases by less, marginally, the more 𝑋  and 𝑌  you have, which isn't the case. I'm also not sure if it would be accurate to say 𝑋  and 𝑌  have increasing opportunity cost, because the cost of both always stay the same.  Is there a name for this kind of factor, and if not, how would you describe it succinctly to get across the idea that having too much of one factor is less optimal than having a balanced amount of both? The situation described, where total output depends multiplicatively on two factors (A = X × Y) and efficiency is maximized by balancing investment between them, is often referred to as exhibiting a **"complementarity"** or **"multiplicative synergy"** among inputs. While neither input alone experiences diminishing returns nor increasing opportunity cost in the traditional sense, the *relative marginal benefit* of increasing one factor depends on the level of the other. This property is sometimes called **relative diminishing marginal benefit** or **interdependent marginal returns**; as one input becomes much larger than the other, additional investment in the larger input yields proportion In the scenario where output is produced by the product of two factors (A = X × Y), each of which can be increased at equal and constant cost, the phenomenon described is not classical diminishing returns nor increasing opportunity cost, since each factor independently exhibits linear returns and constant incremental cost. Instead, the effect is best described as *complementarity with balance optimization*. Governments issue bonds instead of simply raising taxes or printing more money to finance their spending for several reasons. This can cause GDP, particularly per capita GDP, to overstate the economic well-being of residents, as not all of this income remains available for domestic use. Why have bank deposits gone down with rising interest rates?  From an opinion piece in the Washington Post:  "Since the Federal Reserve began to raise rates about a year ago, deposits leaving the banking sector have totaled nearly $1 trillion".  Why would bank deposits go down while interest rates are going up? I thought the higher interest rates would attract more deposits. When the Federal Reserve raises interest rates, it affects the financial system in multiple ways. Although higher rates can sometimes attract more deposits by allowing banks to offer greater returns, they also make alternative investments more appealing. Money market funds and Treasury securities, for instance, often offer even higher yields than traditional savings accounts during periods of rising rates. As a result, consumers and businesses may move their money out of bank deposits in search of better returns elsewhere. Additionally, higher interest rates make borrowing more expensive and can slow economic activity, reducing the amount of new money flowing into bank accounts. This combination of factors explains why, even as banks raise the rates they When the Federal Reserve raises interest rates, it becomes more expensive for banks to borrow money, but it also increases the yields on alternative investments outside traditional bank accounts—such as money market funds, Treasury bills, and other short-term securities. Banks may be slow to raise the interest they pay on savings and checking deposits, so customers seeking higher returns often move their money to these higher-yielding alternatives. As a result, despite higher overall interest rates, deposits at banks can decline, as people transfer funds in search of better returns, leading to the notable reduction in bank deposits mentioned in the Washington Post opinion piece. Although higher interest rates might seem likely to attract more bank deposits, the opposite has happened in recent years. When the Federal Reserve raises interest rates, it drives up yields not only on bank savings accounts but also on alternative investments like money market funds and Treasury securities. These alternatives typically offer even better returns than traditional bank deposits. As a result, many individuals and businesses move their money out of banks to chase higher yields elsewhere. This trend, combined with people withdrawing deposits to cover higher borrowing or living costs in a tighter economic environment, has led to nearly $1 trillion in deposits leaving the banking sector since the Fed began raising rates. In short, When the Federal Reserve raises interest rates, it often increases the rates on new loans and some savings products. However, traditional bank deposit accounts, such as checking and regular savings accounts, may not see their interest rates rise as quickly or as much. At the same time, alternatives like money market funds and Treasury securities start offering significantly higher returns, attracting savers looking for better yields. As a result, many individuals and businesses move their money out of bank deposits to these higher-paying investments. This shift explains why, despite rising interest rates overall, bank deposits have actually declined—totaling nearly $1 trillion in outflows from banks in the past When the Federal Reserve raises interest rates, people might expect bank deposits to increase, since banks could offer higher yields on savings. However, in recent years, deposits have actually fallen despite rising rates—nearly $1 trillion has left the banking sector. This happens because many banks are slow to raise the interest rates they pay on checking and savings accounts, while alternative options like money market funds, Treasury bills, and online banks often respond quicker and offer much higher returns. As a result, savers move their money away from traditional banks in search of better yields elsewhere, leading to a drop in overall bank deposits.

While the death toll was severe, it impacted the entire world. The eruption launched huge amounts of volcanic ash, water and sulphuric acid into the atmosphere, obscuring the Sun and repelling some solar radiation. The Tambora eruption’s volcanic winter hit hard in 1816, which has become known as the Year Without a Summer. Temperatures dropped worldwide and climate and weather changes were felt everywhere. But Europe and North America were arguably the most severely hit, with lakes and rivers being frozen over in July and August. Crop damage was rampant, harvests ruined and food shortages widespread. Food prices skyrocketed and violent riots erupted. Malnourishment soon became a severe issue, facilitating disease epidemics that killed tens of thousands. Could it get worse? The world is already experiencing severe food shortages and rising prices, though these were largely caused by inflation and the Russo-Ukraine War. However, incidents like this, the COVID-19 pandemic and the blocking of the Suez Canal serve to underscore just how fragile the global supply chain is. The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) is an independent U.S. government agency that insures bank deposits up to a set limit. The FDIC is not itself insured by a private company or external agency. If the FDIC’s insurance fund were ever depleted, federal law allows it to borrow from the U.S. Treasury, effectively receiving a government backstop. In an extreme and unlikely scenario where the FDIC and its resources were insufficient to cover losses, it is generally expected that the federal government—including, if necessary, the Federal Reserve—would intervene to protect depositors and maintain financial stability, potentially by creating additional money or offering The FDIC (Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation) insures deposits at member banks up to legal limits. When the Fed buys government bonds, it pays by creating new reserves, which increases the base money (M0) in the banking system. For two goods, one bit is enough since you only need to know which good is preferred. For three goods, you might expect three pairs, but transitivity means not all pairwise comparisons are independent; knowing A>B and B>C implies A>C. The confusion arises from different counting methods. The formula cited in the paper, (n–1)^2 bits, treats each preference as a unit and may make simplifying assumptions about how preferences are encoded. When representing individual preference maps over N goods, the number of "combinatorial bits" required refers to the minimum information needed to specify a complete ranking of the goods. Each "bit" can be thought of as specifying the direction of preference between a pair of goods. In combinatorial terms, there are N*(N-1)/2 unique pairs when considering N goods, so for each pair, we could use a bit to record whether one good is preferred over the other. However, due to the property of transitivity in preferences, not all pairwise comparisons need to be specified independently; some can be logically deduced from others. In the context of preference maps for N goods, each individual's preference ordering can be represented by specifying, for every pair of goods, which one is preferred. For N goods, there are N(N-1)/2 unique pairs. In theory, each pairwise comparison could be stored as a single "bit" indicating a preference direction. However, transitivity (if A > B and B > C, then A > C) means some preferences are implied by others, reducing the number of independent preferences that must be directly stored. The formula mentioned in the paper, (N-1)^2 combinatorial bits, likely arises from an assumption When representing an individual's preference ordering over N goods, one way to count the number of "combinatorial bits" needed is to consider how many pairwise comparisons must be specified to uniquely determine the preference structure. For N goods, there are C(N,2) = N(N-1)/2 possible pairs, and if all pairwise relations were independent, we would need that many bits. However, if we require the overall preference map to be transitive (so, for example, preferring A to B and B to C implies preferring A to C), not all pairwise comparisons are independent—so the minimum number of bits When considering how many “combinatorial bits” are needed to represent a preference map over N goods, confusion can arise due to the difference between the number of independent pairwise comparisons and the total implied orderings. A strict preference ordering over N goods can, in principle, be specified by a complete ranking, which requires the specification of one unique order out of N! possible permutations. However, if we try to encode preferences through pairwise comparisons, we must ensure that these preferences are consistent (i.e., transitive and antisymmetric), which introduces redundancy. Beinhocker says that  A pattern of matter, energy, and/or information has economic value if the following three conditions are jointly met:  Irreversibility: All value-creating economic transformations and transactions are thermodynamically irreversible. Second, taxes promote fairness by redistributing wealth and funding public services that benefit society as a whole. Third, a reliance solely on central bank money creation could undermine trust in the currency and financial system, leading to instability even if inflation does not rise immediately. Update: Adding a great explaination of "pluralistic ignorance" as suggested by @Lason  The term “pluralistic ignorance” was coined to describe the situation in which almost all members of a group privately reject group norms, yet believe that virtually all other group members accept them (Katz and Allport, 1931). Under such situations, individuals predict that they would lose social standing if they behaved as they wished. Behaving against the group norm could result in negative reactions from other ingroup members. Therefore, people are likely to follow perceived group norms to maintain positive impressions in their groups, even when they do not support the norms (Miller and McFarland, 1987; Miller and Prentice, 1994; Prentice and Miller, 1996; Geiger and Swim, 2016). In line with this idea, in situations of pluralistic ignorance, some people even actively enforce the perceived norms (i.e., publicly criticizing a “misfit” into accepting the norm), although they privately disapprove of the norms (Willer et al., 2009). Consequently, public behaviors of groups as a whole do not coincide with the majority of group members' private preferences under circumstances of pluralistic ignorance. Thus, the situation of pluralistic ignorance is well represented in the following sentence: “No one believes, but everyone believes that everyone else believes” (Krech and Crutchfield, 1948). An illustrative example of pluralistic ignorance in economics occurs in financial markets during asset bubbles. Imagine a group of investors who privately believe that a stock’s price is overvalued and unsustainable. However, each investor also believes that most other investors genuinely think the high price is justified. Fearing social or financial backlash for going against the perceived consensus, each investor continues to buy or hold the stock, helping to inflate the bubble. This dynamic—where most participants privately disagree with prevailing behavior but publicly conform due to mistaken beliefs about others' views—exemplifies pluralistic ignorance. When the misperception collapses, the bubble often bursts, An example of pluralistic ignorance in economics can be seen in workplace attitudes toward wage transparency. Imagine a company where most employees privately believe that discussing salaries openly would benefit everyone by reducing pay gaps and promoting fairness. However, because each employee thinks their colleagues actually disapprove of salary discussions—perhaps due to assumed company norms or fear of social sanction—they remain silent about their true preference. As a result, no one challenges the status quo, and the wage secrecy persists, even though the majority are privately dissatisfied with it. This phenomenon reflects pluralistic ignorance: a widespread misperception of group norms leading to collective inaction, despite most individuals quietly rejecting Pluralistic ignorance in economics often appears in the context of financial markets and public policy support. For example, consider a workplace where most employees privately believe that a company’s new bonus structure is unfair, yet think that everyone else supports it. Out of fear of seeming contrary, each employee publicly expresses approval. As a result, the flawed system persists—no one voices their true concerns, and management assumes unanimous support. Similarly, in financial markets, investors might privately doubt the health of a bullish market but continue investing because they believe others are confident. This collective misperception can fuel asset bubbles, as seen before the 2008 financial crisis, An illustrative example of pluralistic ignorance in economics can be found in the context of financial markets and ethical behavior. Consider a situation where employees at an investment bank privately believe that certain aggressive sales practices are unethical or risky, yet they perceive that their coworkers accept, or even expect, such behavior as the norm. This collective misperception can perpetuate harmful behaviors, contributing to market instability or financial scandals. Through open market operations, the central bank may buy government debt to increase the money supply. Does the government need to pay interest on the debt held by the central bank, or does the debt become interest-free? Yes, governments generally pay interest on government debt held by the central bank. When a central bank purchases government bonds through open market operations to increase the money supply, it becomes a bondholder like any other investor. The government continues to owe interest on these bonds at the rate specified when the bonds were issued. However, the central bank is typically owned by the government, and any profits it earns—including interest payments from government debt—are usually returned to the government after covering its operating expenses. Thus, while the government formally pays interest on central bank-held debt, much of that interest effectively returns to the government, making the net cost lower than for When a central bank purchases government debt through open market operations, it acquires government securities such as bonds, which typically pay interest. The government continues to pay interest on these securities, regardless of whether they are held by private investors or the central bank. Thus, government debt held by the central bank does not become interest-free; interest payments are still made according to the terms of the securities. However, central banks often return most of their profits—including interest earned on government debt—back to the government’s treasury, which effectively reduces the government’s net interest cost. Despite this practice, from an accounting perspective, the government remains obligated to pay interest When a central bank acquires government debt through open market operations, the government typically continues to pay interest on that debt, just as it would to private holders. The debt does not become interest-free simply because it is held by the central bank. However, central banks often return most of their profits—including the interest received on government securities—back to the government after covering their operating costs. This arrangement can effectively reduce the government's net interest expense, but the obligation to pay interest on the debt remains in place as a formal matter. When a central bank purchases government debt through open market operations, it typically buys government bonds, which usually pay interest. Even when these bonds are held by the central bank, the government continues to pay interest on them. However, any profits the central bank earns, including this interest, are often returned to the government, since many central banks are public institutions. Thus, while the government formally pays interest on debt held by the central bank, much of this cost is effectively recycled back to the government, meaning the net interest burden may be significantly reduced, but the debt does not become entirely interest-free. When a central bank purchases government debt through open market operations, it typically acquires government bonds that pay interest. The government continues to pay interest on these bonds, regardless of whether they are held by private investors or the central bank. However, any profits the central bank earns, including those from interest on government debt, are usually transferred back to the government as part of the central bank’s remittances. As a result, while the government formally pays interest on the debt held by the central bank, much of this cost is effectively recycled back to the government, making the net interest burden lower than if the bonds were held by private investors.
